title: GRIP XR
subtitle: Caged Element - TBA
description:
GRIP XR is where I am right now, and it's a bit different from previous projects since I'm helping shape the game's direction alongside the Lead Designer and Lead Artist, scoping the project while we run the Kickstarter campaign.

On the tech side, one of the main challenges has been the creation mode, which I'm refactoring to be fully game-mode-agnostic. It originally had dependencies baked in from the previous game, so it meant rethinking the whole architecture rather than patching around the old one. I also rebuilt the spline mesh tool from scratch: the old version was Blueprint-based and pretty rigid, so I designed a new one with a non-destructive workflow that lets us merge spline meshes by chunks.

title: Warhammer 40,000: Speed Freeks
subtitle: Caged Element - 2025
description:
For Warhammer 40,000: Speed Freeks, the tech team consisted of just three people, and given that it's a vehicular combat game with large, non-linear levels, that alone was already quite the challenge. That small team size meant I wore a lot of hats, and honestly, that's how I like it.

One of the most interesting problems was the AI driving system. Navigating vehicles through big, open-ended levels isn't as straightforward as typical character navigation, so we needed both spline-based movement for more structured sections and navmesh-based navigation for free roaming. On top of that, I implemented a dynamic obstacle avoidance system based on context steering. To support all this, I also built two editor tools: one to automatically generate nav links across the level, and another to make editing those nav links easier by hand. All three ended up published as commercial Unreal Engine plugins.

Beyond AI, I worked across platform integration with Steam and PlayFab, achievements and statistics systems, performance optimization, internal architecture, and tools. But probably the feature I'm most proud of is the game's creation mode: players could build custom maps while hosting a live session, with other players seeing the changes in real time and playing as the map took shape.

Doing all of this with such a small tech team was a big challenge, but also incredibly rewarding. Every decision mattered, and you could see your impact everywhere.

title: Tintin Reporter - Cigars of the Pharaoh
subtitle: Pendulo Studios - 2023
description:
Tintin Reporter - Cigars of the Pharaoh was where I shifted into a more graphics-focused role, something I pushed for myself after self-studying graphics programming on the side, which was a nice change of pace. I spent most of my time working on character shaders (hair, skin, eyes, cloth) and water rendering, diving into the research behind each one. I also researched and wrote up performance guidelines and optimization techniques (HLOD, object merging) for the art team, which felt like a nice way to make an impact beyond just my own code.

On the tools side, I built a Python procedural character generation tool for 3ds Max with real-time UI, enabling artists to create NPC variations without programmer involvement, plus an improved version of the cinematics tool I had originally built for Alfred Hitchcock - Vertigo.

As for architecture, the game was built on top of the system I had designed for the studio's previous title, so here it was more about revisiting and evolving it for what this project needed.

title: Alfred Hitchcock - Vertigo
subtitle: Pendulo Studios - 2021
description:
Alfred Hitchcock - Vertigo was a turning point for me. The previous title at the studio was already underway when I joined, so there was only so much I could change. For Vertigo, I got to design and implement the game's architecture from scratch for a team that had grown to around 50 people, which was really satisfying. The goal was to fix the pain points we'd run into on Blacksad and make it easy for everyone to script gameplay independently, and it worked out well: gameplay programmers were able to build scenes on their own without running into architectural issues.

Since everything was new, I also mentored 5 junior programmers, helping them find their way around the system until they could work fully on their own. On top of that, I built a visual tool for designers to create cinematics and conversations using timelines, worked with the animation department to integrate motion matching, collaborated with the audio team, and contributed to gameplay and camera work. A project with a lot of variety, which I really enjoyed.

title: Blacksad: Under the Skin
subtitle: Pendulo Studios - 2019
description:
Blacksad: Under the Skin was my first big project. I was coming from a porting background, so this was where I really got to grow as a developer in a larger production. The team was under 20 people, so shipping a narrative-driven 3D adventure of this scope took a lot of effort from everyone, and that environment pushed me to take on more and more as the team trusted me with it.

I built a new camera system for the game, created a tool to solve a cloth physics issue on cinematic transitions, and another one to manage and automate the dubbing pipeline across 8 languages, which saved us a ton of manual work. I also helped with console optimization, building a runtime object management system that tripled the framerate on consoles from 10 to +30fps in some performance-critical scenes. Over time I got more involved in gameplay and architecture too, and contributed to the porting process, which felt like coming full circle given where I started.

title: Pipe Push Paradise
subtitle: Stage Clear Studios (Porting) - 2018
description: Ported to Nintendo Switch/PS4/Xbox One. I handled platform adaptation, optimization, and certification.

title: Paranautical Activity
subtitle: Stage Clear Studios (Porting) - 2018
description: Ported to Nintendo Switch/PS4/Xbox One. I handled platform adaptation, optimization, and certification.

title: Blacksea Odyssey
subtitle: Stage Clear Studios (Porting) - 2018
description: Ported to Nintendo Switch/PS4/Xbox One. I handled platform adaptation, optimization, and certification.

title: Darkborn
subtitle: Starquake Games - 2017
description:
Darkborn was a master's degree project: a 16-person team building a 3D hack and slash in UE4 that we ended up submitting to the PlayStation Awards. It didn't go anywhere after that, but it was my first real experience working on a game with a sizable team.

I was one of six programmers on the tech side. Together we built the combat system, and individually I worked on camera, physics, some AI, UI integration, tools, and parts of the game architecture. It was the kind of project where everyone pitched in wherever needed.

It wasn't a commercial project, but it's where I first got a taste of what it's like to build something bigger than yourself with a real team, and a lot of how I approach work now traces back to that.

title: XR Research Project
subtitle: DEI UC3M - 2017
description: During my time at UC3M, I worked in a university research department on a cross-reality (XR) project connecting physical and virtual environments through Oculus Rift, Arduino, and a web app. I worked on the VR side using Unity, but my main focus was building the webapp from front to back. It was a node-based visual programming tool that let users design behaviors for Arduino components in the real world and link them to their virtual counterparts in VR, so an interaction on either side would show up on the other.
